women workers Of New York. The
letter follows:


You have doubtless heard of the
shirt waist makers strike out here.
For weeks l have been so deeply
engaged in the struggle with these
striking men and women that |
have found it difficult to think of
anything else. am still in the
fight, as the strike is not yet over
There were between 10000 and
10000 out about there weeks ago.
Now there are about 5000 strikers
left, the rest having gone back, on
the union terms, with recognition of
the union. -s ~,"-


I'VE go about to the striking
shops talking to the girls (they are
overwhelmingly discouraged some
times), and give them talks, confer
with them, etc. Then we picket, go
to see employers, who ask for COM
'1nHtees- from the union; in short,
We do score of different things a
day, andsit takes all of the efficient
time of each day. Last night | went
to the night court, where our pick
cts are brought, arrested purely at
the behest of the masters. who hire
thugs to annoy them and policemen
to haul them into court when they
resent the rough treatment Of the
thugs.


Everywhere the struggle is the
